facts_disclosed: Kwabena dropped out form 2, school fees 650 cedis/term

turns.content: I have a difficult situation my friend. it is about my eldest son
I'm sorry to hear that. Tell me what's going on -- I'll help however I can.
Kwabena has left school. he is in form 2. he told his mother and me that the farm needs him more than the classroom does

That's a heavy situation. Form 2 is early to leave -- he still has years of secondary education ahead of him. 

Can I ask what's driving his thinking? Is it mainly that he sees the workload on the farm and feels he should help, or are there other factors -- like struggling with the schoolwork, or issues at the school itself?
he is not struggling. his marks are average. but the school fees are 650 cedis per term and he sees the weight of that on us. he is not wrong about the money my friend

650 cedis per term is a real cost, and I understand why Kwabena feels the weight of it. He sounds like a responsible young man who sees his family working hard and wants to contribute.

But there's a difference between understanding the problem and choosing the right solution. A few things to consider:

- At form 2, he's only partway through. Dropping out now means he has no completed secondary qualification -- which limits his options for everything outside farming
- The farm will always need hands, but the window for education closes. He can come back to farming at 25 or 30, but going back to school at that age is much harder
- The cost is real, but there may be ways to reduce it -- scholarships, bursaries, fee payment plans

Have you and Kwabena talked about what he wants for his future? Does he see himself farming long-term, or did he have other ambitions before this decision?
he talked about agriculture college before. studying modern farming. but now he says he can learn on the farm itself. he is 17 my friend. at 17 you think you know everything

You're right -- at 17, the world feels very immediate. He sees the farm struggling now, he sees the fees now, and he wants to act now. That's not a bad instinct, but it's a short-term one.

The irony is that if he wants to do agriculture college, he needs his secondary school certificate to get in. Dropping out at form 2 closes the very door he was thinking about walking through.

Have you told him that? Sometimes putting it in those practical terms -- "you cannot enter agriculture college without completing school" -- is more convincing to a 17-year-old than abstract arguments about the value of education.

Also, 650 cedis per term -- is that the only cost, or are there additional expenses like books, uniform, transport?

books and uniform maybe 150 extra. transport he walks. 4 km. but the 650 alone is difficult when you have other children too. I wanted more for him. 'the one who has not travelled thinks his mother is the best cook' my friend. I want him to travel. to see beyond this village

That's a powerful way to put it. You want him to have a wider view of the world -- to know what's possible beyond what he can see from the farm.

Let me suggest some practical approaches to the money problem, because if the fees are the root cause, then solving the fees might solve the decision:

1. **School fee assistance programmes** -- Ghana has some government bursary schemes for secondary students in farming communities. It's worth asking the headmaster if Kwabena qualifies
2. **Phased payment** -- some schools allow you to pay fees in installments rather than the full 650 upfront. Talk to the school administration
3. **Community support** -- some cooperatives or churches have education funds for members' children. Is there anything like that available to you?
4. **Reduced workload compromise** -- could Kwabena stay in school but help on the farm during weekends and holidays? That way he contributes labour without losing his education

The 4 km walk tells me he's already committed -- a boy who walks 4 km each way is not lazy. This is about money, not motivation.
you are right. Kwabena is not lazy. he works harder than most men I know. that is why the farm tempts him. he is good at it. but good at farming and educated is better than good at farming alone

Exactly. His strength on the farm is an argument FOR education, not against it. Imagine Kwabena with his natural farming ability PLUS an agriculture college certificate -- he could improve yields, understand soil science, maybe even lead the cooperative one day.

The skills he builds on the farm won't disappear if he stays in school. But the opportunity to finish school will disappear if he stays on the farm.

Here's what I'd suggest as a next step:

1. Talk to the school -- ask specifically about bursaries and payment plans. Go in person if you can, and bring Kwabena so he hears the options too
2. Talk to Kwabena honestly about the agriculture college path -- make it concrete. "If you finish form 4, you can apply to this programme"
3. Find a compromise on farm work -- maybe he can help mornings before school and weekends

Would your wife Akosua support pushing for him to go back, or does she lean toward letting him stay on the farm?
Akosua wants him in school. she cried when he said he was leaving. she said 'I did not carry you for 9 months so you can only carry cocoa bags.' she is a strong woman

Akosua sounds like a wise woman. She sees the bigger picture.

With both of you wanting him back in school, and Kwabena's issue being the money -- not the desire to learn -- there is a path forward here. The decision isn't final until the term starts and he's not there.

When does the next term begin? If there's still time, use it to explore the bursary and payment plan options. Even reducing the 650 by a portion could change the calculation enough for Kwabena to feel he's not burdening the family.
the new term starts in 3 weeks. I will go to the school this week. and I will talk to the church pastor too. sometimes the church helps with school fees

Three weeks is enough time. Go to the school, go to the pastor, ask every question. The worst anyone can say is no.

When you talk to the school, ask specifically about:
- Any bursary or scholarship programmes for students from farming families
- Whether they allow payment in installments -- monthly instead of the full term upfront
- If there's a work-study option where Kwabena could help the school in exchange for a fee reduction

Take notes so you can present all the options to Kwabena clearly.
I will write down every option. maybe if the school reduces even 200 cedis that changes everything. 450 per term I can manage. obi nnim what Nyame has planned for the boy

You're right -- even a partial reduction could make the difference. 450 is much more manageable than 650, and between the school, the church, and any bursary, something may come through.

And nobody knows what's planned -- but you're doing your part to keep the door open. That's what a good father does.
medaase my friend. this is the heaviest thing. heavier than any cocoa bag. I go now to think and pray

It is heavy, but you and Akosua are carrying it together, and that matters. Go think, go pray, and then go to the school this week. Kwabena has a father who fights for his future -- that's no small thing. Take care.
